item.title: Example Talk
item.description:
Click on the Slides button above to view the built-in slides feature.

Slides can be added in a few ways:

  • Create slides using Wowchemy’s Slides feature and link using slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file
  • Upload an existing slide deck to static/ and link using url_slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file
  • Embed your slides (e.g. Google Slides) or presentation video on this page using shortcodes.

Further event details, including page elements such as image galleries, can be added to the body of this page.

item.title: Autonomous Quadcopter
item.description:
This project was undertaken by the Team Robocon. It is a fully funded by the BITS Alumni Association (BITSAA). We aim to build quadcopter achieving a level 5 autonomy.

I am leading the State Estimation subsystem of the project. Currently, the plan is to fuse the RTAB-Map and the Extended Kalman Filter algorithms to accomplish the goal.

item.title: GenNav
item.description:
GenNav, short for ‘General Navigation’, was undertaken by the Electronics and Robotics Club at BITS Goa. As we did the project during the COVID-19 lockdown, I remotely contributed to the open-source project.

GenNav is a python library for robotics navigation algorithms and utilities commonly required in navigation stacks. The library aims to be completely modular and have a unified API so that it is usable in a broad range of applications and easily extensible to new robotics domains. We further extended the project to construct a ROS Wrapper on top of Gennav, called ‘gennav_ros’. ‘gennav_ros’ can be used if someone wishes to use ‘gennav’ in a ROS based stack. Since the package is open-sourced using PyPi here, GenNav can also be installed using pip install gennav, although installation from source is recommended. Rudimentary documentation for the project can be found here.

As part of the team, I implemented path planning algorithms like Rapidly‐exploring Random Tree (RRT), Rapidly‐exploring Random Graph (RRG), and some utility classes and methods for implementing Graphs, etc. While implementing the algorithms from scratch was challenging (albeit fun), the more important thing to take care of was maintaining the API across all the path planners and making all generalised pieces of code ‘modular’. I also made Jupyter and Google Colab notebook tutorials for showing how to use the available algorithms. The notebooks can be found here.

item.title: Human Authentication System using Forehead Wrinkles
item.description: The project was based on the study of various feature detectors and image matching algorithms applied on the forehead dataset. As a part of Deep Learning course, I built a computer vision based forehead based human authentication on mobile and hand held devices. The project covers the use of SIFT, SURF, ORB, ArcFace, and AKAZE algorithms for matching images. Image Preprocessing (CLAHE) was performed before applying the matching algorithms. I achieved an average accuracy of 59.99%, and best accuracy of 73.997% using the ArcFace algorithm. A genuine-imposter histogram was plotted for each algorithm. The generated genuine and histogram datasets were used to plot the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve of FRR (X-Axis) vs FRR (Y-Axis). Correct Recognition Rate (CRR) and Equal Error Rate (EER) were calculated from the resulting ROC curve. The detailed report of the project can be found here.

item.title: Path Planner and Autonomous Controller for a Robot
item.description:
This is a project based on path planning and control theory of robots. What this means is that I need to plan a path from the given start to goal points through a given environment using one of the path planning algorithms. I then need to create a code for a controller for the robot. This will enable the robot to traverse the path in a ‘point-by-point’ manner to reach the goal. The environment in which my project runs consists of a series of static obstacles and the robot. Trotbot (a robot model) has been used to test my code. Trotbot is based on the Omnibase package. Both are developed by the Electronics and Robotics Club, BITS Goa.

I implemented a PID controller and a Randomly Exploring Random Tree (RRT) path planner for a Trotbot robot. The Trotbot autonomously planned its way through a series of static obstacles and traversed the resultant path. The code was written using Python, and ROS was used as the framework. Each entity with different functionality (e.g.: controller, path planner) was written as a ROS Node. The Publisher-Subscriber, or Pub-Sub, model in ROS was used to set up the communication between the nodes.

Structure of the project:

  1. Obstacle detector node which publishes a fixed list of obstacles. Here I published the list of centre points of the cylindrical obstacles.
  2. Planner node which subscribes to obstacles and publishes a path.
  3. Controller node which subscribes to the path and the current pose of the robot. It calculates the required velocity of the robot, and publishes it on the corresponding topic. This controller will traverse the path one point at a time using PID Controller.

All the code was tested on the robot using simulations on Gazebo. More information about the project can be found here.

P.S.: This project was done as a part of the QSTP Course Project. QSTP (Quark Summer Term Projects) is an annual event where the technical clubs from BITS Goa offer introductory courses in their respective domains. My introduction to robotics was this very project, offered in May 2020 during the COVID-19 lockdown. The course page can be found here. Since it was my first door to the vast field of robotics, this project holds a special position in my heart. I’ll always be grateful to the course mentors, whose efforts are the reason I have come this far in robotics.

item.title: Self Balancing two wheeled robot
item.description:
A strictly two-wheeled robot (without a free castor wheel for support) provides a helpful platform for testing complex control algorithms. It helps ensure that a multi-variable system will be physically stable. In this project, we study the use of the modern Linear Quadratic Regulator controller for balancing the robot and extend the application for the robot to traverse any path. A differential drive mechanism is used while developing the robot, which helps decouple the translation and rotation stages in path traversal. An A* algorithm has been used for path planning in a static obstacle occupied environment. It is shown that an LQR controlled robot can successfully help the robot stabilize on the spot without falling and enable linear motion in the forward direction.

P.S.: This project was done as a part of the Autonomous Mobile Robotics course offered at BITS Pilani. The project report can be found here.

item.title: Big Data for Big Problems
item.description: Head over to this link to read my blog.

item.title: Slides
item.description:
Create slides in Markdown with Wowchemy

Wowchemy | Documentation

Features

  • Efficiently write slides in Markdown
  • 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides
  • Supports speaker notes
  • Mobile friendly slides

Controls

  • Next: Right Arrow or Space
  • Previous: Left Arrow
  • Start: Home
  • Finish: End
  • Overview: Esc
  • Speaker notes: S
  • Fullscreen: F
  • Zoom: Alt + Click
  • PDF Export: E

Code Highlighting

Inline code: variable

Code block:

porridge = "blueberry"
if porridge == "blueberry":
    print("Eating...")

Math

In-line math: $x + y = z$

Block math:

$$ f\left( x \right) = ;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$

Fragments

Make content appear incrementally

{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}}
{{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}}
{{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}}

Press Space to play!

One Two Three

A fragment can accept two optional parameters:

  • class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS)
  • weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears

Speaker Notes

Add speaker notes to your presentation

{{% speaker_note %}}
- Only the speaker can read these notes
- Press `S` key to view
{{% /speaker_note %}}

Press the S key to view the speaker notes!

  • Only the speaker can read these notes
  • Press S key to view

Themes

  • black: Black background, white text, blue links (default)
  • white: White background, black text, blue links
  • league: Gray background, white text, blue links
  • beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links
  • sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links
  • night: Black background, thick white text, orange links
  • serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links
  • simple: White background, black text, blue links
  • solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links

Custom Slide

Customize the slide style and background

{{< slide background-image="/media/boards.jpg" >}}
{{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}}
{{< slide class="my-style" >}}

Custom CSS Example

Let’s make headers navy colored.

Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:

.reveal section h1,
.reveal section h2,
.reveal section h3 {
  color: navy;
}

Questions?

Ask

Documentation

item.title: An example conference paper
item.description:
Click the Cite button above to demo the feature to enable visitors to import publication metadata into their reference management software.
Create your slides in Markdown - click the Slides button to check out the example.

Supplementary notes can be added here, including code, math, and images.
